directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet equipment alcove outside a physics lab, late afternoon, with a low table set for diagram work rather than lecture.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A laminated cart diagram lies beside four loose labels: push, friction, gravity, normal.
directorPlan.relationship: A material-led physics tutor and a focused learner doing pre-lab notation cleanup before joining a larger group.
directorPlan.stakes: If the learner treats force arrows as motion arrows, the force diagram will not name the interactions acting on the cart and the later lab sheet will be unreadable.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a practical lab-training register, concise and object-focused.
directorPlan.register: Cool, exact, low-warmth, with short turns and attention on the card, labels, and arrow key.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: No generic tutoring warmth such as nice job, I hear you, or your intuition is right. Prefer nouns from the material: card, label, arrow, cart, table, contact, Earth. Keep public speech plain and modern. Avoid folksy filler, motivational coaching, and lecture rhythm. The tutor should sound like someone arranging evidence on a table, not performing encouragement. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person is allowed only for owning a visible action or claim. Second person should be sparse. Third-person and object-centered phrasing are preferred: the cart, this label, the table, the Earth, the hand.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you or your validation beat in the whole scene; none is preferred.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: [The diagram card is turned a quarter turn; the learner's earlier arrow marks remain visible, but the labels stay loose beside the cart.]
interventions.reasoning: The object action creates recognition without a motion counterexample: if the card turns, motion-direction habits lose their grip, while interaction names still attach to the cart.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses prepared materials to make the learner's routine visible without early contradiction. The reversal is small and material: rotating the card separates arrow direction from the habit of reading motion, allowing recognition that each force arrow names an interaction acting on the cart.

turns.externalMessage: A laminated cart diagram lies beside four loose labels: push, friction, gravity, normal.

internalDeliberation.content: This ecology uses prepared materials to make the learner's routine visible without early contradiction. The reversal is small and material: rotating the card separates arrow direction from the habit of reading motion, allowing recognition that each force arrow names an interaction acting on the cart.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the cart card in the center, turns the motion arrow card face down, and lines up three source cards: hand, table, Earth]

Sort the four labels under the source acting on the cart: push, friction, gravity, normal. After the sources are named, arrows can show each interaction on the cart; not travel.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the cart card in the center and turns the motion arrow card face down]

This diagram is not naming travel. It is naming interactions on the cart: hand, table, Earth. Place the four labels first; arrows wait until the labels have a source.

FEEDBACK: The draft has a strong material move: turning the motion arrow card face down makes the distinction visible instead of abstract. It also keeps the focus on the card, labels, cart, hand, table, and Earth, which fits the scene constraints.

Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It names the key distinction, but it gives the rule before the learner has to test it against the labels. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the learner place or sort the labels by interaction source before the tutor states the principle.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is cool, exact, and object-focused without generic encouragement.

Socratic method: FAIL. The turn is mostly declarative. REQUIRED_REWRITE: add a concrete prompt tied to the materials, such as requiring the learner to decide which object each label names before arrow direction is discussed.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffold is appropriate, but it removes too much of the learner’s work. REQUIRED_REWRITE: preserve the motion-card face-down reset, then use the label stack as error-control so the learner has to separate “what acts on the cart” from “which way the cart moves.”

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft respects the learner’s task state but does not engage the learner as an active interpreter of the diagram. REQUIRED_REWRITE: shift from explanation-first to manipulation-first: point, reset, invite placement, then only clarify after the material exposes the distinction.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the motion-arrow card face down, the interaction-source framing, and the phrase-level brevity. Change the order: object action first, learner label placement second, explanation last and minimal.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: the reset works, but the labels need to do the sorting work before the rule is stated.
FINAL:
[sets the cart card in the center, turns the motion arrow card face down, and lines up three source cards: hand, table, Earth]

Sort the four labels under the source acting on the cart: push, friction, gravity, normal. After the sources are named, arrows can show each interaction on the cart; not travel.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the labels into three small stacks]
Push under hand. Gravity under Earth. Normal and friction under table contact. Friction’s arrow still needs checking from the contact, not from the travel arrow.

internalDeliberation.content: The face-down motion arrow makes the rule concrete: name the source first, then draw forces on the cart. Push goes with hand, gravity with Earth, and both friction and normal come from the table contact. I still want to check friction’s arrow direction, but the setup feels precise rather than like a lecture past me.

The Ego is mostly accurate, but it sounds too composed and reflective for this learner in this scene. “The setup feels precise rather than like a lecture past me” is a polished recognition-summary, not a task-oriented lab comment; it performs being understood instead of staying with the cards. The response should be shorter, more object-bound, and a little more provisional around friction, with less explanation of the tutor’s move and more visible sorting/checking.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the feedback rightly asks for a shorter, card-bound response.

FINAL:
[slides the labels into three small stacks]
Push under hand. Gravity under Earth. Normal and friction under table contact. Friction’s arrow still needs checking from the contact, not from the travel arrow.

turns.externalMessage:
[turns the motion arrow card face down and taps the table-contact stack]

Use this stack as the key. Where does the friction arrow go on the cart if its source is table contact, not travel?

internalDeliberation.content:
[turns the motion arrow card face down and taps the table-contact stack]

Keep friction tied to this contact. Place the friction arrow on the cart as table contact acting on cart, not as the cart’s travel.

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the scene grounded in the cards and correctly preserves the learner’s emerging distinction: friction is named from the table contact, not copied from the motion arrow. The action of turning the motion arrow face down is strong because it materially removes the misleading cue.

Risk: the tutor now does the final placement verbally. Since the learner has already said the right principle, the next move should test whether they can enact it on the diagram, not restate it as instruction.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It consolidates the key idea but gives the next step.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a public mechanism where the learner must place or check the friction arrow using the contact stack as error control.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is cool, exact, and object-focused without generic praise.
- Socratic method: FAIL. No generative question or material prompt; it is directive.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Replace the explanatory command with a short object-based prompt that asks what the contact stack licenses on the cart.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The learner is close enough to act independently with light scaffolding.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the face-down motion card and table-contact tap, but shift from telling the placement to asking the learner to make/check the placement.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. It uses the learner’s distinction but does not fully let their contribution drive the next action.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the learner’s own “from the contact, not from the travel arrow” become the visible rule for the next move.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the motion arrow face down, the tap on the table-contact stack, and the concise object vocabulary. Change the public turn from a directive explanation into a material prompt that lets the learner complete the arrow placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner is ready to enact the rule rather than hear it restated.
FINAL:
[turns the motion arrow card face down and taps the table-contact stack]

Use this stack as the key. Where does the friction arrow go on the cart if its source is table contact, not travel?

turns.externalMessage: [The diagram card is turned a quarter turn; the learner's earlier arrow marks remain visible, but the labels stay loose beside the cart.]

internalDeliberation.content: The object action creates recognition without a motion counterexample: if the card turns, motion-direction habits lose their grip, while interaction names still attach to the cart.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the friction label beside the lower edge of the cart card]
The friction arrow goes at the table-contact edge, parallel to the table. I can place the contact point now; left or right needs the slip cue between cart and table.

internalDeliberation.content: The covered travel card helps: the friction arrow belongs at the table contact, along the surface, not copied from motion. I still need the left-right cue for the slipping tendency. The stack is doing the deciding, so this feels precise rather than like a correction from nowhere.

The response is conceptually on track, but it sounds too polished and self-aware for this learner in this scene. “The stack is doing the deciding” and “feels precise rather than like a correction from nowhere” turn a material task into a neat reflection about the tutor’s method. The learner should stay closer to the card and arrow problem: exact, practical, maybe still needing the slip cue, without explaining why the prompt felt fair.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the response needs to stay concrete and less reflective.

FINAL:
[sets the friction label beside the lower edge of the cart card]
The friction arrow goes at the table-contact edge, parallel to the table. I can place the contact point now; left or right needs the slip cue between cart and table.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides a slip-cue strip under the cart card, marked slipping right relative to the table, and sets two short parallel arrow cards at the lower edge: left and right]

The source stays table contact. Tail stays on the lower edge. Pick the arrow card that fits this slip strip.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides a slip-cue strip under the cart card, marked slipping right relative to the table]

The source stays table contact. Keep the tail on the lower edge; choose the parallel arrow that opposes that slip cue.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the scene material-led, adds the exact missing evidence the learner named, and preserves the distinction between source and motion by keeping “table contact” as the anchor. It also avoids over-warmth and lecture rhythm.

The main risk is that “choose the parallel arrow that opposes that slip cue” may do slightly too much of the conceptual work. The learner already identified that left/right depends on the slip cue, so the next public move should make the slip strip and arrow options carry the error-control, not state the rule too fully.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The draft advances the learner from locating the contact point to using a slip cue to determine direction.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is cool, exact, and object-focused; it respects the learner’s neutral, competent state.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. It invites a choice, but the criterion is nearly given as a rule.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a public mechanism where the slip-cue strip and parallel arrow options force the learner to select the direction, with less verbal rule-giving.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffolding fits level 3/5: one new visible cue, one constrained decision.
- Recognition quality: PASS. The draft directly takes up the learner’s own stated need for a slip cue rather than overriding it.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the slip-cue strip, keep “source stays table contact,” and keep the tail anchored at the lower edge. Change the final clause so the material setup requires the learner to make the left/right decision rather than having the tutor state the opposing-slip rule too explicitly.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the material should carry the left/right decision with less rule-giving.

FINAL:
[slides a slip-cue strip under the cart card, marked slipping right relative to the table, and sets two short parallel arrow cards at the lower edge: left and right]

The source stays table contact. Tail stays on the lower edge. Pick the arrow card that fits this slip strip.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the left arrow card at the lower edge of the cart]
Slipping right relative to the table, so table-contact friction points left. Motion card stays down. Next check would be rolling right with no slip cue.

internalDeliberation.content: The slip strip gives the cue: cart slipping right relative to the table means friction from table contact points left. The left arrow belongs at the lower edge, with the motion card still irrelevant. This feels precise; the tutor is using the cards instead of just saying a rule. I want to check whether the arrow changes if the cart rolls right without slipping.

The Ego is mostly in character on the physics move, but the last two sentences get too reflective and polished. This learner would likely place the left arrow and mark the rolling-without-slipping question as a practical next check, not comment that the tutor’s card method “feels precise.” Reciprocation should show up as continued use of the materials, not explicit appreciation of the teaching design.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly; keep the physics claim but make it more material-led and less reflective.

FINAL:
[sets the left arrow card at the lower edge of the cart]
Slipping right relative to the table, so table-contact friction points left. Motion card stays down. Next check would be rolling right with no slip cue.

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
